does: This paper provides a theoretical analysis of weak-to-strong generalization in the context of neural network feature learning. Using a two-layer network model where a strong student model with pre-trained representations organized into low-dimensional subspaces is fine-tuned on outputs from a weaker task-specialized teacher, the authors prove that multi-step SGD enables the student to efficiently learn the teacher's task while preserving its diverse pre-trained capabilities. The key contribution is demonstrating that weak-to-strong training achieves genuine feature learning—the student acquires novel task-relevant feature directions through gradient descent rather than simply activating pre-existing features—while maintaining orthogonal off-target features that constitute general knowledge.
connects: This work builds on recent proposals using weak-to-strong generalization as a scalable approach to superhuman AI alignment, extending prior theoretical research that either assumed fixed student representations or analyzed only simplified settings. It addresses a gap left by existing analyses regarding whether realistic multi-step stochastic gradient descent can simultaneously achieve feature learning and preserve pre-trained capabilities. The paper connects to broader literatures on representation learning in neural networks, the geometry of learned features in structured settings, and the mechanisms underlying transfer learning and fine-tuning. It also engages with questions about how neural networks maintain diverse capabilities while specializing on new tasks.
enables: By establishing theoretical foundations for weak-to-strong generalization in the feature-learning regime, this work opens pathways for developing scalable alignment techniques for increasingly capable AI systems. The analysis suggests conditions under which weaker supervisory signals can effectively guide stronger models toward desired behaviors without catastrophic forgetting of general abilities. This could inform practical approaches to aligning large language models and other superhuman systems where obtaining strong ground-truth labels is infeasible. The theoretical framework may also enable design of training procedures that better preserve capability diversity while specializing on safety-relevant tasks, and could support investigation of what geometric properties of representations make them amenable to weak supervision.
